---
title: "Ch 6 — Percentage and Interest"
subtitle: "Per-hundred — and the simple-vs-compound interest difference that built modern finance"
lesson-id: "kar.percentage"
tier: "kar"
kiselev-book: "Arithmetic"
kiselev-chapter: 6
kiselev-chapter-title: "Percentage and interest"
kiselev-sections: "§§161–185 (percentage definition, simple interest, compound interest, applications)"
prerequisites: [kar.decimals, kar.ratio-proportion]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: [databases, research, ai-ml]
estimated-minutes: 45
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Are decimals and their operations automatic? See
   [`kar.decimals`](../04-decimals/index.qmd).
2. Can you set up and solve a *rule of three* problem? See
   [`kar.ratio-proportion`](../05-ratio-proportion/index.qmd).
3. Are you comfortable that $25\% = \dfrac{25}{100} = 0.25$?
4. Can you compute $30\%$ of $200$ in your head? *(Answer: $60$.)*

If three yeses, proceed.
:::

## Touch

A percentage is a fraction with denominator $100$. Saying *"$15$
percent"* is the same as saying *"fifteen per hundred"* — i.e.
$\dfrac{15}{100} = 0.15$.

```{ojs}
viewof base = Inputs.range([0, 1000], {value: 200, step: 10, label: "base amount"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof pct = Inputs.range([0, 200], {value: 25, step: 1, label: "percentage (%)"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const part = base * pct / 100;
  return `**${pct}%** of ${base} = ${part}.

(That is, ${pct}/100 = ${(pct/100).toFixed(2)} as a decimal, times ${base}.)`;
}
```

Three classical questions: (a) what is *$p\%$ of $A$*? (b) *what
percent of $A$ is $B$*? (c) *if $p\%$ of something is $C$, what is
the something?* All three reduce to a single proportion.

## Wonder

1. The phrases *"increased by $20\%$"* and *"$120\%$ of the
   original"* — are they the same?
2. *"Discount $10\%$ then a further $20\%$"* vs *"Discount $30\%$"*
   — same final price?
3. **Simple interest:** the interest is the same every year.
   **Compound interest:** the interest itself earns interest. How
   different are the two over $20$ years?
4. The famous **rule of $72$:** at $r\%$ per year compounded
   annually, money doubles in roughly $72/r$ years. **Why $72$?**

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

(1) **Yes** — both equal $1.2 \cdot $ original. (2) **No** —
two successive discounts are *multiplicative* ($0.9 \times 0.8 =
0.72$, i.e. $28\%$ off), not additive ($30\%$ off). (3) Compound
trumps simple at any positive rate; at $5\%$ over $20$ years, simple
gives $100\%$ extra, compound gives $\sim 165\%$ extra. (4) From
$\ln 2 \approx 0.693$, the doubling time at rate $r$ is $\ln 2 / \ln
(1 + r/100) \approx 0.693 / (r/100) = 69.3 / r$. The "$72$" is a
slight over-estimate that's friendlier for mental math (because
$72$ has many divisors).
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Definition

A **percent** ($\%$) is a fraction with denominator $100$.
$p\% = \dfrac{p}{100}$. Express $35\%$ and $7\%$ as decimals.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$35\% = 0.35$; $7\% = 0.07$. *(Arithmetic §161.)*
</details>

### Problem 2 — Three classical questions

Solve each via the proportion $\dfrac{\text{part}}{\text{whole}} =
\dfrac{p}{100}$:

(a) What is $15\%$ of $80$?

(b) What percent of $40$ is $30$?

(c) If $20\%$ of a number is $36$, what is the number?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
(a) $\dfrac{x}{80} = \dfrac{15}{100}$, so $x = 12$.

(b) $\dfrac{30}{40} = \dfrac{p}{100}$, so $p = 75$. **$30$ is
$75\%$ of $40$.**

(c) $\dfrac{36}{N} = \dfrac{20}{100}$, so $N = 180$.

*(Three sides of one proportion. **The unifying setup is the rule
of three.**)*
</details>

### Problem 3 — Increase and decrease

A quantity is *increased* by $p\%$. Show the new value is the
original times $\left(1 + \dfrac{p}{100}\right)$. *Decreased*:
times $\left(1 - \dfrac{p}{100}\right)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Original $A$. Increase by $p\%$: add $p\%$ of $A = \dfrac{p A}{100}$.
New value: $A + \dfrac{p A}{100} = A \left(1 + \dfrac{p}{100}\right)$.

Symmetrically for decrease. *(Arithmetic §165.)*
</details>

### Problem 4 — Successive percentages

A discount of $10\%$ followed by another $15\%$: what is the
*combined* discount percentage?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Multiply: $(1 - 0.10) \cdot (1 - 0.15) = 0.9 \cdot 0.85 = 0.765$.
That's $76.5\%$ of the original — i.e. $23.5\%$ off.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$0.9 \cdot 0.85 = 0.765$. **Combined discount: $23.5\%$** (not
$25\%$).

*Two successive percentage changes are **multiplicative**, not
additive.* This is one of the most consequential rules in finance.
</details>

### Problem 5 — Simple interest

**Simple interest** $I$ on principal $P$ at rate $r\%$ per year for
$t$ years is $I = \dfrac{P r t}{100}$. The total amount $A = P + I
= P\left(1 + \dfrac{r t}{100}\right)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Each year, the interest is $\dfrac{P r}{100}$ — same every year
(the *principal* is constant). After $t$ years, total interest is
$\dfrac{P r t}{100}$. Total amount: $P + I$. *(Arithmetic §170.)*

*Example:* $P = 1000, r = 5, t = 3$: $I = 150, A = 1150$.
</details>

### Problem 6 — Compound interest

**Compound interest** *adds* the interest to the principal at the
end of each period — so subsequent interest is computed on the
new (larger) total. Show the formula: after $t$ periods at rate
$r\%$ per period, $A = P \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)^t$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
After year $1$: $P \cdot (1 + r/100)$. Treat this as the new
principal for year $2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
After 1 period: $P \cdot (1 + r/100)$.
After 2: $P \cdot (1 + r/100)^2$.
... After $t$: $P \cdot (1 + r/100)^t$.

**$A = P (1 + r/100)^t$.** *(Arithmetic §175.)*

*Example:* $P = 1000, r = 5, t = 3$: $A = 1000 \cdot (1.05)^3 =
1000 \cdot 1.157625 = 1157.63$. (Compare simple at the same
parameters: $1150$. Compound earns $7.63$ more in $3$ years.)
</details>

### Problem 7 — Reverse questions

A bank advertises *"$10\%$ APR, compounded annually."* If you
deposit $₹1000$, after how many years will you have $₹2000$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$1000 \cdot (1.1)^t = 2000$, so $(1.1)^t = 2$, i.e. $t = \log_{1.1}
2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$t = \dfrac{\log 2}{\log 1.1} \approx \dfrac{0.693}{0.0953} \approx
7.27$ years.

**Rule of $72$:** $72 / 10 = 7.2$ years — close to the exact
$7.27$. *(Arithmetic §178 — the *doubling-time approximation*.)*
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Percent ($\%$)**: a fraction with denominator $100$. $p\% =
\dfrac{p}{100} = $ the decimal $0.0p$.

**The three percentage questions** all reduce to:
$$\dfrac{\text{part}}{\text{whole}} = \dfrac{p}{100}.$$

**Increase / decrease.** New value $= $ Original $\times \left(1
\pm \dfrac{p}{100}\right)$.

**Multiplicativity of successive percentages.** Two successive
$p\%$ and $q\%$ changes give a combined factor of $\left(1 \pm
\dfrac{p}{100}\right)\left(1 \pm \dfrac{q}{100}\right)$ —
**not** additive.

**Simple interest:** $A = P (1 + r t / 100)$, where $r\%$ is the
yearly rate and $t$ years.

**Compound interest:** $A = P (1 + r/100)^t$.

**Rule of $72$:** doubling time $\approx 72/r$ at rate $r\%$
compounded annually.

:::

A verse:

> *Percent is per-hundred — fraction or decimal, your choice.*
> *The three classical questions reduce to one proportional voice.*
> *Successive changes multiply — never add — that's the rule of finance.*
> *And compound interest crushes simple, given enough years to
> dance.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> A shirt costs $₹800$ after a $20\%$ discount. Original price?

If $80\%$ of original $= 800$, then original $= 800 / 0.8 = 1000$.

**Original: $₹1000$.**

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Compute $40\%$ of $250$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$0.4 \times 250 = 100$.
</details>

**W2.** What percent of $80$ is $32$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$32/80 = 0.4 = 40\%$.
</details>

**W3.** Increase $200$ by $25\%$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$200 \times 1.25 = 250$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Compute the simple interest on $₹5000$ at $8\%$ per year
for $3$ years.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$I = 5000 \cdot 8 \cdot 3 / 100 = 1200$. **$₹1200$.**
</details>

**P2.** Compute the compound interest on $₹5000$ at $8\%$ per year
for $3$ years (compounded annually).

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$A = 5000 \cdot (1.08)^3 = 5000 \cdot 1.259712 = 6298.56$.
$I = A - P = 1298.56$. **$₹1298.56$.**

(Compare: simple interest gives only $₹1200$. The extra $₹98.56$
is the "*interest on interest*.")
</details>

**P3.** A car loses $15\%$ of its value each year. After $4$
years, what fraction of its original value remains?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(0.85)^4 = 0.5220$. **About $52.2\%$ remains.**
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** A salesman raises his price by $20\%$ then offers a
$20\%$ discount. *Net effect on the buyer*?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$1.20 \cdot 0.80 = 0.96$. **The buyer pays $96\%$ of the original
— i.e. $4\%$ off.** (Not $0\%$, despite the symmetric percentages.)

*The asymmetry: $20\%$ of a *larger* number is more than $20\%$
of a smaller. The buyer is worse off than $20\%$ off the original
would have been.*
</details>

**S2.** *Continuous compounding* approaches $A = P e^{r t / 100}$
(Algebra II Ch 7). Compute the "advantage" of continuous
compounding over annual at $r = 5\%$ for $t = 10$ years.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Annual: $(1.05)^{10} \approx 1.6289$.
Continuous: $e^{0.5} \approx 1.6487$.

Continuous beats annual by about $0.0198$, i.e. about $2\%$ more
total amount. *(For higher rates and longer times, the gap
widens. At $r = 10\%$ for $30$ years: annual $17.45 \times$,
continuous $20.09 \times$.)*
</details>

### Kiselev-style problems

Three problems in Kiselev's terse style, adapted from
*Arithmetic*, §§170–185.

**K1.** A merchant sells a watch at $₹1200$ and makes a $20\%$
profit. What was the cost price?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$1.20 \cdot \text{cost} = 1200$. So cost $= 1000$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Cost price: $₹1000$.** Profit: $₹200$ ($= 20\%$ of $1000$).
</details>

**K2.** Population grows at $2\%$ per year. After how many years
does it triple?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$(1.02)^t = 3$, so $t = \log 3 / \log 1.02 \approx 1.0986/0.0198
\approx 55.5$ years.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
About $55.5$ years.
</details>

**K3.** A loan of $₹1000$ at $5\%$ compound annual interest is
repaid by *equal annual payments* over $3$ years. Find the annual
payment.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The present value of $3$ equal payments $X$ at $5\%$ should equal
the loan: $X \left(\dfrac{1}{1.05} + \dfrac{1}{1.05^2} +
\dfrac{1}{1.05^3}\right) = 1000$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{1}{1.05} + \dfrac{1}{1.05^2} + \dfrac{1}{1.05^3} \approx
0.9524 + 0.9070 + 0.8638 = 2.7232$. So $X = 1000/2.7232 \approx
367.21$. **Annual payment: $₹367.21$.**

*(The classical *amortisation formula*; underpins all loan
calculation.)*
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**Source pointer.** Adapted from Kiselev, *Систематический курс
арифметики* (1884; modern reprint Fizmatlit). Russian original:
[archive.org scan](https://archive.org/details/libgen_00032025).
The simple/compound interest distinction (Problems 5, 6) is
§§170–175; the rule of $72$ (Problem 7) is mentioned in §178 as a
practical mnemonic.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Two successive $10\%$ discounts equal one $20\%$ discount."**
   *Wrong.* They equal $19\%$ off ($0.9 \cdot 0.9 = 0.81$).
2. **"Simple and compound interest are the same."** Compound
   exceeds simple by an *exponentially growing* amount over time.
   At long horizons, the gap is enormous.
3. **"$200\%$ is impossible."** Percentages can exceed $100$ —
   *"$200\%$ of $50$"* means *twice $50$, i.e. $100$*. There's no
   ceiling.
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you state and apply the *three classical percentage
   questions* (Problem 2) without notes?
2. Did the *multiplicative successive percentages* idea (Problem 4
   and S1) feel like a key insight?
3. Can you derive the compound interest formula by iterating one
   year at a time (Problem 6)?

- **3 yes:** **You have completed Kiselev *Arithmetic* in
  Russian-classical style.** Continue to *Algebra Part I* —
  signed numbers and beyond.
- **2 yes:** redo Problem 6 — compound interest is the chapter's
  payoff.
- **0–1 yes:** practise the three classical percentage questions
  (Problem 2) on five different problems.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`kar.decimals`](../04-decimals/index.qmd) — percentages are
  decimals scaled by $100$; the multiplicative-successive rule is
  decimal multiplication.
- [`kar.ratio-proportion`](../05-ratio-proportion/index.qmd) — the
  rule of three underlies the three classical percentage
  questions.

### What this unlocks

- [`ka1.signed-numbers`](../../tier-kiselev-1-algebra-1/01-signed-numbers/index.qmd) —
  the natural next step in Kiselev: signed numbers and algebraic
  manipulation.
- [`ka2.exponentials-logarithms`](../../tier-kiselev-2-algebra-2/04-exponentials-logarithms/index.qmd) —
  compound interest is exponential growth, formalised.
- [`ka2.sequences-progressions`](../../tier-kiselev-2-algebra-2/05-sequences-progressions/index.qmd) —
  GPs underlie compound interest; the amortisation formula in K3 is
  a GP sum.
- [`t1.ratio-proportion`](../../tier-1-foundations/09-ratio-proportion/index.qmd) —
  same content, t-tier idiom.

### Kiselev chapter alignment

- **Arithmetic — Chapter 6: Percentage and interest (§§161–185).**
  Russian original on
  [archive.org](https://archive.org/details/libgen_00032025).
- **Sister synthesis lesson:**
  [`t1.ratio-proportion`](../../tier-1-foundations/09-ratio-proportion/index.qmd) —
  the synthesis Tier 1 covers ratio + percentage in one lesson.
- **Read alongside the source PDF** for Kiselev's full development
  of *amortisation* and *annuities* (§§180–185).

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Databases.** **Financial systems** track every account using
percentage-of-balance and compound-interest logic. Bugs here cost
money — the percentage formulas in this lesson are sometimes
hard-coded into core banking platforms.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** **Exponential population dynamics** — bacteria,
viruses, populations — follow the same compound-growth formula
($A = P \cdot e^{r t}$ in continuous time). The doubling-time rule
of $72$ is the everyday epidemiologist's tool.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** **Learning rate decay schedules** in neural network
training are often "*reduce by $p\%$ every $k$ epochs*" — a
percentage-of-percentage rule, exactly Problem 4.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones:** Kiselev (the §§161–185 ordering); Bhāskara (the
  *Līlāvatī* interest problems); Fibonacci (1202 — the *Liber
  Abaci* introduced compound interest to Europe).
- **The compound interest formula** is the most-applied formula in
  human commerce. Memorise it.
- **What this lesson does not do:** treat *continuous compounding*
  ($A = P e^{r t}$) in full — that's an Algebra II Ch 7 topic
  (the limit of compound interest as compounding frequency → ∞).
- **Arithmetic — track complete.** Six chapters, all aligned to
  Kiselev's *Систематический курс арифметики* (1884). Together
  with Algebra I (9), Algebra II (7), Planimetry (9), and
  Stereometry (5), the Kiselev track now spans **36 lessons**
  across all five books.
:::
